title: Inquiry summary - How the European Commission dealt with an administrative complaint concerning rights of persons with disabilities (2266/2024/ET)
text: Disclaimer: The case has been closed. The decision will not be published, to protect the personal data of the persons concerned. The Ombudsman is publishing this summary document instead of the full decision. Suggestions for improvement The European Ombudsman has asked the European Commission to improve its support for staff members with disabilities to comply with the United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UNCRPD), to which the European Union is party. The Ombudsman’s suggestions for improvement arise from a complaint-based inquiry that examined how the European Commission handled a request for assistance submitted under the Staff Regulations that raised allegations of psychological harassment linked to disability-related concerns. The Ombudsman has asked the Commission to reinforce the role of its central disability team as a focal point for disability-related issues, by enabling it to contribute to a more holistic handling of disability-related complaints and concerns. This should include not only its current role in providing support in the context of requests for reasonable accommodation, but also the capacity to provide specialised expertise and guidance where requests or complaints, whether framed under Article 24 or Article 90 of the Staff Regulations, raise issues of disability or alleged disability-related discrimination. To this end, the Commission should reflect on how to develop clearer, more holistic mechanisms for addressing disability-related complaints. These mechanisms should ensure that services responsible for handling disability-related complaints and harassment allegations systematically consult the central disability team - or other staff with expertise in disability matters - before finalising decisions in cases that contain disability elements. Such an approach would help ensure that disability-related grievances are considered in their broader context and in a manner consistent with the Commission’s obligations under the UNCRPD. In parallel, the Ombudsman suggested that the Commission ensure that its internal guidance to staff clearly explains the role of the central disability team in situations where a staff member with a disability encounters difficulties with the implementation of reasonable accommodation or perceives discriminatory treatment. This guidance should clarify how the central disability team can support staff in navigating available procedures and seeking effective redress. Background and Ombudsman’s assessment The case concerned a complaint by a staff member of the European Commission. The complainant argued that the Commission had failed to ensure the effective implementation of reasonable accommodation measures, leading to unequal treatment and harassment. After the Commission rejected the staff member’s request for assistance under Article 24 of the Staff Regulations and a subsequent administrative complaint under Article 90 of the Staff Regulations, the staff member turned to the European Ombudsman. Under the Staff Regulations, a staff member may submit a request for assistance where they allege acts amounting to harassment. In this case, the request for assistance contained allegations of psychological harassment. The Commission therefore examined the request under its anti-harassment framework and assessed it in accordance with the applicable legal standards, including the requirement that a staff member seeking assistance must provide prima facie evidence, or a “beginning of proof”, of harassment. The Ombudsman found that the Commission applied the relevant framework and that there was no indication of a manifest error in its conclusion that the legal threshold required to establish a beginning of proof of harassment had not been met. At the same time, the Ombudsman examined the disability-related issues raised in the case. The European Union is bound by the United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities, which requires the EU administration to ensure non-discrimination and equal opportunities for persons with disabilities, including through the provision of reasonable accommodation. These obligations underline the need to examine disability-related discrimination claims to an appropriate legal standard. The inquiry found that the Commission did take note of the disability issues raised insofar as they were relevant to assessing whether harassment had occurred. However, this examination was necessarily limited to the specific question of whether those elements could establish a beginning of proof of harassment. The Ombudsman considered that the correct application of the anti-harassment framework does not exhaust the Commission’s obligations where allegations of disability discrimination are concerned. The case also raised broader questions concerning the procedural framework available under the Staff Regulations. Requests for assistance concerning alleged harassment are examined under the anti-harassment framework, while disputes concerning administrative decisions affecting a staff member, including decisions relating to reasonable accommodation, may be challenged through the procedures provided for administrative requests or complaints. However, these procedures are not designed to address broader patterns of alleged disability discrimination that are not linked to a specific and identifiable decision on reasonable accommodation. As a result, evidence of discrimination may need to be examined through separate challenges to individual acts, rather than through a holistic assessment of alleged discrimination occurring over time. The Ombudsman also noted that disability-related workplace situations may involve a range of processes, including medical opinions, mediation efforts, administrative decisions and appeals. Such processes can become lengthy and burdensome. While some accommodation measures may lend themselves to objective assessment, others, particularly those concerning working practices, require sustained dialogue and may be less amenable to enforcement through isolated formal decisions. While the Commission has taken measures aimed at fulfilling its obligations under the Convention and strengthening support for staff members with disabilities by reinforcing its central disability team and mediation capacity in recent years, the case illustrates that clearer procedural arrangements could help ensure that disability-related grievances are considered in their broader context. In this respect, the Ombudsman considered that greater procedural clarity and visibility regarding how disability discrimination complaints are handled is an important element in ensuring that remedies are genuinely accessible and effective in practice, in line with the Convention and made suggestions for improvement in this regard.
summary: Disclaimer: The case has been closed. The decision will not be published, to protect the personal data of the persons concerned. The Ombudsman is publishing this summary document instead of the full decision.
